So, the antithesis of the seven deadly sins of email copywriting are the seven things that every email actually needs. The first thing is a big idea. You need to go into writing an email with a most wanted response. I call it the MWR. I know that's very clever. You need to know, what am I trying to accomplish in this email? Do I want them to click on a link to go back to the copy? Do I want them to watch a video? Do I want them to actually click to buy? Know what your most wanted response in this email is and focus on that single objective. The second thing every email needs is a story of some kind. But it needs to be a story that puts people in a little mental movie of them actually enjoying or benefiting from your product. So it could be as simple as saying, you know, Joe came in this morning with his new puppy and he was at his wits end because the puppy will not sleep through the night and keeps doing his business in the house. I was able to direct him to two solutions that are going to solve both those problems for him. Do you have a puppy? And now you're into the copy portion of your email, but you told a little story to set that up. It's very important. The third thing every email needs is a compelling subject line. This is what gets people to open the email or causes them to skip it. The subject line of your email is like the headline of your sales copy. Its job is one thing and one thing only to get people to open the email and read it. The fourth thing every email needs is a unified through line. What this really means is you need to make sure the story you're telling, the subject line that gets them to open the email and the closing portion that asks them to click on a link to go back to your website. You need to make sure those things are all consistent with one another. So if you have a pet store and you have somebody come in and ask about help with their puppy who's doing his business in the house and you have a product that helps with that, all those things need to match up in your email. Make sure the story actually relates to what you're selling in the email. The fifth thing every email needs is a clear, concise, singular voice. No we speak, no corporate talk. It's an email from a single person with a name which leads to the sixth thing every email needs which is it needs the feeling that it's coming from a friend. And I just want to point out to you if you open your inbox right now and look at the emails that you opened today and read, probably none of them had in the headline or in the subject line the word sale or 30% off. Probably none of them have a big logo or banner at the top of the email. It doesn't look like an ad and probably none of them are written in we speak. The emails that you have in your inbox that are from a friend that you read have a certain look and feel. So I recommend making the emails that you send out to the people on your list look like an email from a friend. And finally number seven is you need a single, clear call to action. One link asking one thing of the reader and that needs to be consistent with your most wanted result. That's why it's important to know that from the beginning so you know what response you're asking for, what call to action you're making at the end of the email. And these are the seven things that every email needs. So how do we master the art of writing email copy? The key here is to think of it as mastery. It happens over time, but I can give you seven elements to focus on that will really help you grow your skill as an email nurturing sequence copywriter. And the first one is read spam subject lines. I know that sounds a little crazy, but the truth is spammers are really good at getting you to open your emails. I'm not suggesting that you spam people do not do that. That's wrong and it's annoying and it's illegal too. But what I am suggesting is learn from people who are good at what they do and spammers are good at getting you to open your email. And now one word of caution, be very careful which spam emails you open. I think it'll be obvious to you if you look in your spam folders, which one you should not go near. Just be careful out there. The second step I'm going to recommend in mastering email copy is read magazine headlines. If you look at the magazines on the stand as you're checking out at the grocery store, you'll notice they have some very interesting headlines. They make you want to pick up the magazine. Maybe they even make you pick it up and buy it. Maybe you even bought the National Enquirer because you saw a weird headline. You wanted to read the story or maybe you didn't, but maybe you noticed that the person in front of you in line bought the National Enquirer. These people who write these headlines are some of the most highly paid copywriters in the business because they have about four to seven words in which to convince somebody to make an impulse buy of a magazine or newspaper. So it's a good place to study great headlines or in the case of email copy, subject lines. You can even borrow and reword some concepts from magazine covers and come up with some pretty strong subject lines for your emails. The third step in mastering writing email copy is to write in a conversational voice. You want to write the way you talk. You want to write the way you would write to a friend or to a family member in an email. And that's probably going to be a little less formal and a little less business like and more like person to person, which is exactly the tone you want to communicate in your emails. It doesn't mean you have to be unprofessional. It just means you need to loosen up a little bit. Now these next four tips, I'm going to go ahead and fess up. I stole them from Ernest Hemingway, but they're his four rules of writing and they will serve you well in writing email copy especially. The fourth step to mastering email copy is to use short words. You know, people give your email about two seconds before they decide whether they're going to read it or they're going to delete it. One way to make sure they delete it is to use long words. So don't do that. The fifth point to mastering email copy is to use short sentences. The shorter the sentences, the better in fact because people are scanning that email usually in a preview pane on their email client and they read the first two to three lines and if you don't capture their interest and get them moving along in the copy really quickly, they're never going to read the rest of the email. So using short words and sentences is very important and short paragraphs. In fact, I like to make my paragraphs as often as I can one sentence. I know the English lit majors out there are cringing right now, but this is a different form of writing. It's email writing, it's email copy and you need to adopt the techniques that work best in this medium. And in my opinion, using really short paragraphs is key. Finally, in your email copy, you want to use active language, not the passive voice. So the passive voice is when the subject of the sentence is acted upon. The ball was hit by Don. That's a lot less engaging than Don hit the ball. There's a lot more vigorous feeling in that sentence than there is in the first one. So make sure you use active language. Those are the seven keys to mastering writing email copy and this is how you write an email nurturing campaign.
